title: Quantum Ergodicity and Mixing on Large Schreier Graphs
speaker: Cyril Letrouit
abstract: In this talk, I will present joint work with Charles Bordenave and Mostafa Sabri establishing quantum ergodicity and quantum weak mixing for sequences of finite Schreier graphs converging, in the Benjamini–Schramm sense, to an infinite Cayley graph whose adjacency operator has absolutely continuous spectrum. The proof relies on a new approach to quantum ergodicity on graphs, based on trace computations, resolvent approximations and representation theory.

proof_stages.label: Spectral convergence for bounded-degree graphs
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up bounded-degree graphs, adjacency operators, Benjamini-Schramm convergence to a rooted homogeneous infinite graph, and the resulting weak convergence of empirical spectral measures.

proof_stages.label: AC spectrum as delocalization and quantum limits
proof_stages.summary: The spectral decomposition of the infinite limit is linked to dynamics via the RAGE intuition, motivating delocalization, quantum ergodicity, and the stronger quantum-mixing correlation condition.

proof_stages.label: Cayley-Schreier approximation framework
proof_stages.summary: The finite graphs are specified as Schreier graphs of permutation representations of a finitely generated group, with weak convergence of the representations characterized by normalized traces and equivalent to Benjamini-Schramm convergence to the Cayley graph.

proof_stages.label: Weak-convergence theorem with decorrelated observables
proof_stages.summary: Under weak convergence, pure AC spectrum with bounded spectral density on an interval, and an asymptotic self-correlation decay condition on the observables, the theorem gives quantum ergodicity and microscopic quantum mixing in that interval.

proof_stages.label: Strong-convergence theorem for all observables
proof_stages.summary: To remove the special observable hypothesis, the second theorem assumes strong convergence of the representations, rapid decay for the group, an AC interval, and a resolvent-decay condition, yielding quantum ergodicity and quantum mixing for arbitrary bounded observables.

proof_stages.label: Nonvacuity checks and extensions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the assumptions can be verified in concrete non-tree group families and that a matrix-valued version extends the results beyond regular Schreier graphs.

proof_stages.label: Trace-identity reduction of the proof
proof_stages.summary: A basic identity rewrites averaged eigenfunction correlations as a trace involving functional calculus; Benjamini-Schramm convergence converts this to group-correlation terms, which are then controlled either by observable decorrelation or by strong convergence plus rapid decay and resolvent estimates.

proof_stages.label: AC-spectrum hypothesis as an open spectral problem
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion emphasizes that the theorems assume absolutely continuous spectrum, while proving the nature of spectra for Cayley graph operators remains a major open mathematical-physics problem.

examples.note: The Z^d/torus family shows that quantum ergodicity can hold in a periodic setting while quantum mixing fails, motivating the need for growth/expansion and the resolvent-decay condition.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks how the results behave for Z^d/torus-type approximations, noting which assumptions hold or fail and reasoning via exponential/cosine eigenfunctions why quantum mixing fails
